mods:
There are various translation for the two terms dang lu 當壚 and Huji 胡姬 into English, which we can both read in Li Bai’s another poetry with the title 前有一樽酒行二首:
春風東來忽相過，金樽淥酒生微波。
落花紛紛稍覺多，美人欲醉朱顏酡。
青軒桃李能幾何，流光欺人忽蹉跎。
君起舞，日西夕。
當年意氣不肯傾，白髮如絲嘆何益。
琴奏龍門之綠桐，玉壺美酒清若空。
催弦拂柱與君飲，看朱成碧顏始紅。
胡姬貌如花，當壚笑春風。
笑春風，舞羅衣，君今不醉將安歸？
Edward H. Schafer writes on 27:
The zither plays “The Green Paulownias at Dragon Gate,”
The lovely wine, in its pot of jade, is as clear as the sky.
As I press against the strings, and brush across the studs, I’ll drink with you, milord;
“Vermilion will seem to be prase-green” when our faces begin to redden.
That Western houri with features like a flower——
She stands by the wine-warmer, and laughs with the breath of spring
Laughs with the breath of spring,
Dances in a dress of gauze!
“Will you be going somewhere, milord, now, before you are drunk?” In the footnote n125 on p. 284 he further writes: “[...] ‘Western houri’ is hu chi [hu ji], which in Chinese connotes a beautiful lass, with gracious manners, of Western or Northern, probably Iranian, origins. [...]”. See:
Charlie Ng and Phoebe Poon translated the terms as “This exotic lady” and “At the wine table”, see 132, while Kidder Smith and Mike Zhai wrote “A Turkic singing girl” and “[tends] the wine pot”. See 21.
Meanwhile, Owen in writes “Turkish wench” and “bar”.
130
Ballad of Youth (second of two)
A young man of Five Barrows suburb
east of the Golden Market,
Silver saddle and white horse
cross through wind of spring.
When fallen flowers are trampled all under,
where is it he will roam?
With a laugh he enter the tavern
of a lovely Turkish wench.
[08045]
少年行二首之二
五陵年少金市東
銀鞍白馬度春風
落花踏盡遊何處
笑入胡姬酒肆中
369
4. Ho [Hu] 胡 (a general appellation for Central Asians, Turks, Tartars, etc.) were often associated with tavern keeping (e.g., 02628) but Li Po’s line speciafically echoes lines from Hsin Yen-nien’s [Xin Yannian 辛延年] “Officer of the Guard” [Yulin lang] 羽林郎 (CHs 2.15b):
Relying on our general’s power,
We tease the Turk of the tavern.
The Turkish wench is just fifteen,
On a spring day she stands alone at the bar.
依倚將軍勢
調笑酒家胡
胡姬年十五
春日獨當壚
Since Ishida writes in his sub-chapter that he interprets the Huji 胡姬 in most of the cases meaning Iranian peoples (Sogdians), possibly living among, or already inter-mixed with, Mongol-Turkic peoples in the Northwest, I would suggest we don’t use the exclusive form “Turkish” or “Turkic singing”, but stick to Schafer’s broader term “Western”.
